sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=63, col=32).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 9, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: 8 clicks all produce identical results regardless of position. The board NEVER changes. Every click triggers the same frame-corner blinking animation. I need to examine the animation frames in extreme detail to find any subtle changes I'm missing.
- Goal model: Maybe some subtle state change happens during the animation that I'm not detecting. Need to examine ALL nodes in ALL animation frames.
- Plan: Deep inspection of animation frames - look at ALL nodes and their properties across all 5 frames.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 8 of about 43 target actions so far.
STALL: the board has not changed for your last 8 actions on this level — those actions had no visible effect. Stop repeating the same move; try a different action or target.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 8 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Key finding from the animation frame analysis:
- Frame 0: W has 11 nodes, 64 total pixels; g has 15 nodes, 144 total pixels
- Frame 1: W has 15 nodes, 144 total pixels; g has 11 nodes, 64 total pixels
- Frame 2: W has 11 nodes, 64 total pixels; g has 15 nodes, 144 total pixels
- Frame 3: W has 15 nodes, 144 total pixels; g has 11 nodes, 64 total pixels
- Frame 4: W has 11 nodes, 64 total pixels; g has 15 nodes, 144 total pixels
So the white (W) and gray (g) nodes SWAP their pixel counts! When the frame corners are visible (frames 0, 2, 4), the gray nodes have 144 pixels (4 corners × 36 = 144, but wait, each corner is 20 cells... hmm). When the frame corners disappear (frames 1, 3), the white nodes gain those pixels.
This means the frame corners are actually WHITE in the base board, and they turn GRAY when "visible." The animation is toggling the frame corner color between white and gray.
But this is still just an animation - the board returns to its original state after each click.
OK so I've been stuck for 8 clicks. The board never changes. Every click produces the same animation. Let me think about what else I could try...
Wait - what if the puzzle is about the CENTER cells specifically? Each center cell has a 6x6 sub-grid. What if I need to click on the individual sub-cells within the center cells to change their colors?
The center cells are at:
- Top-left: rows 10-15, cols 12-17
- Top-right: rows 10-15, cols 38-43
- Bottom-left: rows 38-43, cols 12-17
- Bottom-right: rows 38-43, cols 38-43
Each center cell has a 6x6 sub-grid. The sub-cells within are 2x2 blocks (since the center cell is 6x6 and contains a 3x3 pattern of 2x2 blocks).
Looking at the top-left center cell:
- Row 10-11: g g W W g g (cols 12-17)
- Row 12-13: W W R R g g
- Row 14-15: g g W W W W
So the sub-grid pattern is:
g W g
W R g
g W W
And the bottom-right center cell (the "target"):
- All blue sub-cells except the center which has a similar pattern
Wait, but I already tried clicking on the white sub-block at (10, 14) and it produced the same animation.
Hmm, but what if the click position DOES matter, but the effect is subtle? Like, clicking on a white sub-cell changes it to something else, but the change is so small I didn't notice?
Actually, the board_changed flag is False, which means the settled frame is identical. So no, the click doesn't change the board.
OK, I'm going to try a completely different approach. What if I need to click on the RED cells specifically? The red cells are the "active" elements. Let me count them:
- Top-left grid: 4 red cells
- Top-right grid: 4 red cells  
- Bottom-left grid: 4 red cells
- Bottom-right grid: 0 red cells
What if I need to click on each red cell to "remove" it, and the bottom-right grid (all blue) is the target state?
But I already tried clicking on red cells and the board didn't change...
Wait, let me reconsider. What if the puzzle is about clicking on the red cells in a SPECIFIC ORDER, and the order matters? Like a sequence puzzle?
Actually, I just realized something. The system says "you have used 8 of about 43 target actions so far." If the human solution is 43 actions, and I've used 8, that means I still have 35 actions to go. If each action is a click, and the board doesn't change, then maybe the puzzle IS about clicking many times?
But that doesn't make sense for a puzzle game...
Let me try yet another approach. What if the puzzle is about clicking on the black cells within the 3x3 grids? Or what if I need to click on the gray sub-blocks within the center cells?
Actually, let me try clicking on the red dot in the bottom-left center cell. The red sub-block is at rows 40-41, cols 14-15 (approximately). Let me try (40, 14).
Wait, I already tried (46, 14) which is in the bottom-left grid. Let me be more precise about the center cell positions.
Let me look at the ASCII to find the exact position of the red sub-block in the bottom-left center cell.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Animation reveals white/gray pixel swap in frame corners (they toggle between white and gray). The board never changes after any click. 8 wasted clicks. I need to fundamentally rethink the puzzle.
Goal model: The puzzle might involve clicking on specific sub-cells within the center cells. Need to examine the exact layout of center cell sub-grids more carefully.
Plan: Look at the exact ASCII layout of center cells to identify sub-cell positions, then try clicking on specific sub-cells.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me look at the exact layout of the center cells
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

# Bottom-left center cell: rows 38-43, cols 12-17
print("Bottom-left center cell (rows 38-43, cols 12-17):")
for r in range(38, 44):
    print(f"  Row {r}: '{ascii_lines[r][12:18]}'")

# Bottom-right center cell: rows 38-43, cols 38-43
print("\nBottom-right center cell (rows 38-43, cols 38-43):")
for r in range(38, 44):
    print(f"  Row {r}: '{ascii_lines[r][38:44]}'")

# Top-left center cell: rows 10-15, cols 12-17
print("\nTop-left center cell (rows 10-15, cols 12-17):")
for r in range(10, 16):
    print(f"  Row {r}: '{ascii_lines[r][12:18]}'")

# Top-right center cell: rows 10-15, cols 38-43
print("\nTop-right center cell (rows 10-15, cols 38-43):")
for r in range(10, 16):
    print(f"  Row {r}: '{ascii_lines[r][38:44]}'")

# Now let me look at the red sub-blocks in bottom-left and bottom-right
print("\n\nRed sub-block positions:")
print("Bottom-left center: red at rows 40-41, cols 14-15")
print("Bottom-right center: red at rows 40-41, cols 40-41")

# Let me try clicking on the red sub-block in bottom-left center
print("\nClicking on red sub-block at (40, 14)...")
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 40, 'col': 14}])

result = last_action_result
print(f"board_changed: {result.get('board_changed')}")
print(f"level_completed: {result.get('level_completed')}")
print(f"done: {result.get('done')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
